narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of first-time bar exam success based on academic and background factors from past students, estimated a 55% chance that you would not pass the bar on your first attempt—a threshold that leads to rejection to ensure incoming classes are positioned for strong outcomes. As a Black male applicant, your profile includes a Year 1 school decile of 4, a Year 3 decile of 3, high family income, an LSAT score of 26, an undergraduate GPA of 2.80, and plans to attend full-time, which is the norm for most applicants. The model's decision hinged primarily on three key factors, ranked by their influence on your specific prediction. Your LSAT score stood out as the most powerful driver, strongly pushing the prediction toward bar failure because it falls well below the typical score of around 37 seen in the dataset of past students. In contrast, your high family income provided a notable counterbalance, nudging the prediction toward success by suggesting resources that often support better preparation and outcomes. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.80, lower than the average of 3.21, offered some offsetting positive influence but not enough to overcome the LSAT's weight. Compared to typical students who pass the bar, your early and later school deciles are also on the lower side of average, though they played lesser roles here. This combination means your academic indicators, especially the LSAT, signaled higher risk relative to peers who succeed. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to or above the average, as that single change could dramatically shift future predictions in your favor. Strengthening your overall GPA through additional coursework or a post-bac program might help too. Many applicants in similar situations reapply successfully after targeted improvements—reach out to admissions for feedback on bolstering your profile. Your full-time commitment is a solid foundation, and with persistence, you can align your strengths more closely with what the model sees in high-success candidates.
